返回本批次

有时候，有权势的人也会做出蠢事

Sometimes powerful people just do dumb shit

www.joanwestenberg.com 作者 zdw 分数 59 评论 6 2026/04/14 11:04
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 本新闻稿可免费阅读，并且将保持这种状态。但如果您想要更多 - 每月额外的帖子、没有赞助的 CTA、访问社区以及直接向我提问 - 付费订阅为 2.50 美元/月。很多人告诉我这是值得的。升级 1812 年 6 月，拿破仑·波拿巴率领 685,000 名士兵进军俄罗斯，这是迄今为止欧洲历史上集结的最大规模的军事力量，也是有史以来最大的军事混乱之一。他没有为他们提供连贯的补给计划，也没有明确的时间表来说明俄罗斯人何时会同意按照他的条件进行一场决定性的战斗，他甚至无法为他的赌博阐明一个连贯的目标，除了以某种模糊的方式击败俄罗斯人之外。包括他自己的外交部长塔列朗在内的多位顾问都警告过他，入侵俄罗斯是一个灾难性的想法——但他还是这么做了。到了 12 月，他的士兵约有 40 万人死亡，其中大部分死于饥饿、暴晒以及野战手术的后果，另有 10 万人被俘。欧洲大陆上最令人畏惧的战斗力量——大军团，以冰冻、支离破碎的残骸，艰难地渡过涅门河。对于拿破仑来说，这是终结的开始，他再也无法派出一支像他在毫无意义的俄罗斯之行中浪费掉的那样规模/质量的军队了。从任何合理的角度来看，拿破仑都是一个天才——一个改写了欧洲战争规则的军事头脑，一个在35岁之前从科西嘉小联盟贵族一路奋斗到法国皇帝和大部分现代欧洲统治者的政治操盘手，以及一个围绕司法制度和自由主义思想的改革家。但这些都没有阻止他做出任何领导者所做出的最愚蠢的决定之一，因为他很傲慢，因为他长期以来一直逍遥法外，以至于他把自己的运气与一个制度混为一谈，而且因为（塔列朗除外）他周围的大多数人都不再对他说“不”。有一种特殊的人不能从表面上接受这个故事，你已经遇到过他们。我绝对确定这一点。他们出现在每个评论部分和回复帖子中，其中某个有权势的人做了一些表面上看起来像错误的事情 - 他们的论点总是以同样的方式进行：你不明白，这实际上是一个更大计划的一部分，这里有一个你和我看不到的策略，因为我们没有在那个指定和提升的水平上运作......他们是 4D 国际象棋人群。他们他妈的无处不在。当埃隆·马斯克在 2022 年 10 月以 440 亿美元收购 Twitter 时，他本人在放弃尽职调查后试图放弃这个价格（这一决定令人困惑，以至于主审法官凯瑟琳·麦考密克在法庭上公开对此感到惊讶），4D 国际象棋分析师立即兴奋不已。他们坚持说你还没有看到蜂窝的内部！你不明白！你不是地球上最富有的人 - 你怎么可能希望处理他的才华呢？大规模裁员摧毁了公司的无障碍团队和内容审核人员，显然，同样具有战略意义。让某人冒充礼来公司（Eli Lilly）并通过有关免费胰岛素的虚假推文来抬高其股价的验证失败必须是 The Plan™️ 的一部分。广告商的流失导致公司收入大幅下降，而马斯克只是摆脱了沉重的负担，创造了新的东西，玩了一个我们任何人都无法理解的长期游戏。但 2026 年的陪审团发现马斯克在收购过程中故意误导投资者。他解雇的人必须在几周后重新雇用，因为在他们被解雇之前，没有人费心去检查他们是否在管理任何重要的事情。在他的领导下，该公司损失了大约 80% 的价值——因为没有 4D 国际象棋。只是一个亿万富翁，习惯了成为他走进的每个房间里最聪明的人，他甚至没有自己的塔列朗来暗示这可能是一个糟糕的选择，他一时冲动购买了一家公司，然后通过一系列与表面上看起来一样糟糕的决定让情况变得更糟。同样的事情也发生在特朗普身上；每一次混乱的新闻发布会，每一项相互矛盾的政策声明都会立即被他最阿谀奉承的支持者重新定义（奇怪的是，还有某种类型的对手，他们想相信自己正在对抗一个策划者）。“他在分散你的注意力。”“他在控制新闻周期。”“他正在淹没这个区域。”“他完全知道自己在做什么。”“醒醒吧，羊羔。”我同意你——有时特朗普确实知道他在做什么做。有时，挑衅是经过深思熟虑的，而愤怒确实是有目的的。但 4D 国际象棋人群无法区分这些时刻和那些时刻，最简单的解释就是一个 79 岁的老人拿着电话，无法控制冲动，和一群观众数以百万计的人在凌晨 2 点发布他想发布的任何愚蠢的狗屎。我称之为隐藏计划理论。权力者如果不特别，就不会变得强大，对吗？如果他们很特别，如果他们比我们其他人更聪明，他们所做的一切都必须有一个原因，对吗？如果我们看不到那个原因，那一定是我们的问题 - 凡人 - 而不是神圣任命的泰坦，对吗？对吗？！这个最新条目类型是 OpenAI 收购 TBPN，这是由约翰·库根 (John Coogan) 和乔迪·海斯 (Jordi Hays) 主持的每日科技脱口秀节目。据报道，OpenAI 为观看 YouTube 上拥有 58,000 名订阅者的节目支付了数亿美元。该节目向 OpenAI 的首席政治特工 Chris Lehane 汇报。不出所料，合理化者们已经排队了。《财富》杂志发表了一篇题为“OpenAI 花费数亿购买每日科技节目 TBPN 并不完全疯狂的 3 个原因”的文章。争论归结为：OpenAI 正在购买影响力，通过叙事控制包装发行，将自己定位为塑造有关人工智能的公众对话，而此时对话将决定公司运营的监管环境。看起来，其中一些可能是正确的。但值得先坐下来阅读更简单的内容。一家公司的高管告诉员工停止追逐“支线任务”并专注于核心人工智能模型开发，在播客上花费了数亿美元。 CNBC 的头条称其为“追逐共鸣”。Slate 称其为“低俗”。Stratechery 的 Ben Thompson 做了最彻底的拆除工作。他将 OpenAI 比作“彩虹尽头的短途巴士”，这很有趣，也很残酷，但也很正确。整篇 Stratechery 文章都值得一读，因为 Thompson 实际上费尽心力地阐述了 OpenAI 的策略已经变得多么不连贯——他们本来就反对广告，直到广告突然成为他们的计划；Apple 是合作伙伴，直到他们挖走了 Jony Ive；Anthropic 在那里运送模型，而 Sam Altman 正在为脱口秀节目签支票。汤普森的要点是：“没有太多证据表明任何人都知道他们在做什么，也没有任何总体计划。”这就是问题所在。4D 国际象棋读物要求你相信奥特曼 - 谷歌在他的脖子上，人类在他的脖子上，元在他的脖子上 - 坐下来并认为一个订阅人数少于大多数中型游戏流媒体的脱口秀是数亿美元的最佳用途。无聊的读物要求你相信首席执行官做了一些有利于他的自我的事情。选择需要较少信仰飞跃的一项。我知道我要读哪一本……为什么人们抗拒枯燥的阅读？梅尔文·勒纳有一个理论。他于 1980 年出版了一本名为《公正世界的信念》的书，他的论点是，我们大多数人都内心深处需要相信人们得到了他们应得的东西。如果一个人很有钱，那么他一定很聪明。如果他们很聪明，他们的决定一定是有意义的。如果他们的决定看起来很愚蠢，那么，你一定是那个错过了什么的人。这是世界观的温暖毯子。它只是无法在与现实的接触中幸存下来。还有其他事情正在发生，而且它不那么智力/更多动物性。我们随处可见模式。当他们不在的时候我们就能看到他们。卡尼曼的一半职业生涯都建立在这一点上——我们如此渴望在噪音中找到信号，以至于我们会从无到有地构建整个叙述，而一个强大的家伙领先 12 步的叙述比他搞砸的故事更好，因为这就是人们所做的。但 4D 国际象棋框架也让信徒感到高兴。如果你能看到其他人都忽略的隐藏策略，那么你就是聪明的人，你就是那个掌握它的人。当你意识到它的代价时，这就不那么有趣了……当你坚持认为一个有权势的人采取的每一个行动都是大战略的一部分时，你就剥夺了责任，你就不可能把一个错误的决定称为一个错误的决定。每一次失败都会成为未来永远不会到来的成功的准备，每一次丑闻都会分散人们对一场永远不会实现的更大游戏的注意力。球门柱完全消失了，因为框架变得不可证伪；任何结果都可以吸收到理论中。如果这个计划成功了，那就太天才了。如果没有，真正的计划还没有被揭晓。这就是个人崇拜的维持方式——通过解释，通过一群信徒，他们会做智力劳动来理解无意义的事情，他们把混乱视为自己有限理解的证据，而不是他们所看到的东西实际上是混乱的证据。一个人爬得越高，周围的人就越少会反对。他们越富有，他们的坏想法就越能得到资助而不是受到挑战。越成功。他们变得越来越相信自己的成功来自于技能，而不是来自于一些不稳定的、不可重复的技能混合物，时机、运气和其他人的劳动。拿破仑很聪明。到了 1812 年，他还被那些厌倦了与他争论的法警和一个懂得同意更安全的法院所包围，而入侵俄罗斯恰恰是当一个聪明人失去了让他们保持聪明才智的反馈机制时所发生的事情。马斯克购买 Twitter 不是 4D 象棋。OpenAI 以可以资助一个中型人工智能研究实验室的价格购买一个播客并不是一个他妈的战略主线。有时有权势的人只是这样做愚蠢的狗屎，有时根本没有计划。讽刺的是，那些愿意为 4D 国际象棋错觉付出最高代价的人，恰恰是那些最热衷于它的人；因为如果你不能看着一个有权势的人的决定并说“这是一件该死的愚蠢的事情”，你就无法从他们的错误中学到任何东西，你也无法清楚地看到世界。但是，当你要在大声疾呼和看着一个不受约束的狂妄自大的士兵在没有毛皮大衣的情况下将685,000名士兵带进俄罗斯的冬天之间做出选择时，清晰度是唯一值得拥有的东西。
  2. Westenberg 是由我的独立机构 Studio Self 设计、建造和资助的。联系我并与我一起工作： 与我一起工作

Original Summary

  1. This newsletter is free to read, and it’ll stay that way. But if you want more - extra posts each month, no sponsored CTAs, access to the community, and a direct line to ask me things - paid subscriptions are $2.50/month. A lot of people have told me it’s worth it. Upgrade In June 1812, Napoleon Bonaparte marched 685,000 soldiers into Russia - the largest military force ever assembled in European history up to that point, and one of the largest military fuckups of all time.He had no coherent supply plan for feeding them, he had no realistic timeline for when, exactly, the Russians would agree to fight a decisive battle on his terms, and he couldn’t even articulate a coherent goal for his gamble, beyond ~beat the Russians in some vague way.He had been warned by multiple advisors, including his own foreign minister Talleyrand, that invading Russia was a catastrophic idea - and he did it anyway.By December, roughly 400,000 of his soldiers were dead, mostly from starvation and exposure and the consequences of field surgery, and another 100,000 had been captured. The Grande Armée, the most feared fighting force on the continent, clawed its way back across the Niemen River as a frozen, shattered remnant of itself. It was the beginning of the end for Napoleon, who would never again be able to field an army of the size // quality he squandered on his pointless excursion into Russia.Napoleon was, by any reasonable accounting, a genius - a military mind who rewrote the rules of European warfare, a political operator who fought his way up from being a minor league Corsican nobility to the Emperor of France and ruler of most of modern Europe before he turned 35, and a reformer whose ideas around the judicial system and the liberal order still echo today.But none of that stopped him from making one of the dumbest decisions any leader has ever made, because he was arrogant, because he’d gotten away with so much for so long that he confused his luck for a system, and because (with the exception of Talleyrand) most of the people around him had simply stopped telling him no.There’s a particular kind of person who can’t accept that story at face value, and you’ve met them. I am absolutely sure of it. They show up in every comment section and reply thread where someone powerful does something that looks, on its face, like a mistake - and their argument always runs the same way: you don’t understand, this is actually part of a larger plan, there’s a strategy here that you and I can’t see because we’re not operating at that annointed and elevated level…They’re the 4D chess crowd.And they are fucking everywhere.When Elon Musk bought Twitter in October 2022 for $44 billion, a price he himself had tried to back out of after waiving due diligence (a decision so baffling that the presiding judge, Kathleen McCormick, openly marveled at it in court), the 4D chess analysts fired up immediately. You haven’t seen the inside of the honeycomb, they insisted! You don’t get it! You’re not the richest man on earth - how could you possibly hope to process his brilliance?The mass layoffs that gutted the company’s accessibility team and its content moderation staff were, obviously, equally strategic. The verification fiasco that let someone impersonate Eli Lilly and tank their stock price with a fake tweet about free insulin had to be part of The Plan™️. The advertiser exodus that cratered the company’s revenue was just Musk shaking off the dead weight, building something new, playing a longer game than any of us could understand.But a jury in 2026 found Musk liable for deliberately misleading investors during the acquisition. People he’d fired had to be re-hired weeks later because nobody had bothered to check whether they were, you know, running anything important before they were shown the door. The company lost roughly 80% of its value under his ownership - because there was no 4D chess. There was simply a billionaire who’d gotten used to being the smartest person in every room he walked into, who didn’t even have a Talleyrand of his own to hint that it might be a bad call, who bought a company on impulse, and then made it worse through a series of decisions that were exactly as bad as they looked from the outside.The same thing plays out with Trump; every chaotic press conference, every contradictory policy announcement is immediately reframed by his most sycophantic supporters (and, weirdly, by a certain type of opponent who wants to believe they’re up against a mastermind).“He’s distracting you.”“He’s controlling the news cycle.”“He’s flooding the zone.”“He knows exactly what he’s doing.”“Wake up, sheeple.”I’ll grant you - sometimes Trump does know what he’s doing. Sometimes a provocation is calculated and the outrage does serve a purpose. But the 4D chess crowd can’t distinguish between those moments and the moments where the simplest explanation is just that a 79-year-old man with a phone, no impulse control, and an audience of millions is posting whatever dumb shit he feels like posting at 2 AM.I call it the Hidden Plan Theory.The powerful don’t get to be powerful without being special, right?And if they’re special, if they’re smarter than all the rest of us, everything they do must be for a reason, right?And if we can’t see that reason, that must be a problem with us - mere mortals - not the divinely appointed titans, right?Right?!The most recent entry in this genre is OpenAI’s acquisition of TBPN, the daily tech talk show hosted by John Coogan and Jordi Hays. OpenAI reportedly paid in the low hundreds of millions of dollars for a show with 58,000 subscribers on YouTube. The show reports to Chris Lehane, OpenAI’s chief political operative. And predictably, the rationalizers have lined up.Fortune ran a piece titled “3 reasons OpenAI buying daily tech show TBPN for hundreds of millions isn’t totally crazy.” The argument boiled down to: OpenAI is buying influence, packaging distribution with narrative control, positioning itself to shape public conversation about AI at a moment when that conversation will determine the regulatory environment the company operates in.And look, some of that might be true.But it’s worth sitting with the simpler read for a second.A company whose own executives told staff to stop chasing “side quests” and focus on core AI model development spent hundreds of millions of dollars on a podcast. CNBC’s headline called it “chasing vibes.”Slate called it “sleazy.”Ben Thompson at Stratechery did the most thorough demolition job. He compared OpenAI to “the short bus at the end of the rainbow,” which is funny and also brutal and also correct. The whole Stratechery piece is worth reading because Thompson actually bothered to lay out just how incoherent OpenAI’s strategy has become — they were against ads until suddenly ads were the plan, Apple was a partner until they poached Jony Ive, and Anthropic is over there shipping models while Sam Altman is signing checks for a talk show. Thompson’s takeaway: “there just isn’t much evidence that anyone knows what they are doing or that there is any sort of overarching plan.”And that’s the rub.The 4D chess read asks you to believe that Altman - Google breathing down his neck, Anthropic breathing down his neck, Meta breathing down his neck - sat down and decided a talk show with fewer subscribers than most mid-tier gaming streamers was the best possible use of hundreds of millions of dollars.The boring read asks you to believe a CEO did something that served his ego. Pick whichever one requires less of a leap of faith. I know which one I’m going with...Why do people resist the boring read? Melvin Lerner had a theory. He published a book in 1980 called The Belief in a Just World, and his argument was that most of us walk around with a bone-deep need to believe that people Get What They Deserve. If someone is rich, they must be smart. If they’re smart, their decisions must make sense. And if their decisions look dumb, well, you must be the one who’s missing something. It’s a warm blanket of a worldview. It just doesn’t survive contact with reality.There’s something else going on, too, and it’s less intellectual // more animal. We see patterns everywhere. We see them when they’re not there. Kahneman built half his career on this - we are so desperate to find signal in the noise that we’ll construct entire narratives out of nothing, and a narrative where the powerful guy is playing 12 moves ahead is just a better story than one where he fucked up because that’s what people do.But the 4D chess framing also flatters the believer. If you can see the hidden strategy that everyone else is missing, you’re the smart one, you’re the one who gets it. Which rather stops being funny when you realize what it costs…when you insist that every action a powerful person takes is part of a grand strategy, you strip away accountability and you make it impossible to call a bad decision a bad decision.Every failure becomes a setup for a future success that never arrives, and every scandal a distraction from a larger game that never materializes. The goalposts disappear entirely, because the frame has become unfalsifiable; any outcome can be absorbed into the theory. If the plan works, it was genius. If it doesn’t, the real plan hasn’t been revealed yet.This is how cults of personality sustain themselves - through interpretation, and through a community of believers who will do the intellectual labor of making sense of the nonsensical, who treat confusion as evidence of their own limited understanding rather than evidence that the thing they’re looking at is, in fact, confused.The higher someone climbs, the fewer people around them will push back.The richer they get, the more their bad ideas get funded instead of challenged.The more successful they become, the more they start to believe that their success came from skill rather than from some volatile, unrepeatable cocktail of skill, timing, luck, and other people’s labor.Napoleon was brilliant. He was also surrounded, by 1812, by marshals who were tired of arguing with him and a court that had learned it was safer to agree, and the invasion of Russia was precisely what happens when a brilliant person loses the feedback mechanisms that kept them brilliant.Musk buying Twitter wasn’t 4D chess.OpenAI buying a podcast for a price that could have funded a mid-sized AI research lab wasn’t a strategic fucking masterstroke.Sometimes powerful people just do dumb shit, and sometimes there is no plan.The people who will pay the highest price for the 4D chess delusion are, ironically, the people most devoted to it; because if you can’t look at a powerful person’s decision and say “that was a bloody stupid thing to do,” you can’t learn anything from their mistakes, and you can’t see the world clearly.But when the choice is between speaking up and watching an unchecked megalomaniac march 685,000 soldiers into a Russian winter without a fur coat in sight, clarity is the only thing worth having.
  2. Westenberg is designed, built and funded by my solo-powered agency, Studio Self. Reach out and work with me: Work with me

评论（默认中文）

sfink 2026/04/14 13:46
我觉得人们知道如何做人，以及他们周围的人如何做人，但他们从未见过但只在电视或模因帖子中听说或见过的人？完全没有线索。

当然，我们知道 COMPANY_NAME_HERE 的首席执行官必须一次只穿一条腿，但相似之处仅此而已。他们是不同的，他们不会上我们所上当的愚蠢把戏。他们起床时不会遇到困难，也不会担心孩子在做什么。他们的首席执行官配偶不会要求他们倒垃圾或考虑购买哪种酸奶。

另一方面，如果他们做了坏事，那是因为他们是邪恶的。这是一种深深的黑暗邪恶，完全不同于我们周围人的平庸的跛行。当有人拉动他们的链条并且他们变得很激动时，他们不会做出愚蠢的事情。他们为什么要这么做，他们周围都是金钱和其他有权势的人，还有仆人整天向他们灌输聪明的见解。他们所做的一切都是经过计划和计算的，他们会详细地考虑他们对人们造成的伤害。

地球上只有一种人类，而且我们都蠢得一塌糊涂。

I kind of feel like people know how to human, and how the humans around them human, but someone they've never met but only heard about or seen on TV or in meme posts? No clue at all.

Sure, we know the hotshot CEO of COMPANY_NAME_HERE has to put on his pants one leg at a time, but the similarity ends there. They're different, they won't fall for the stupid tricks we fall for. They don't have trouble getting out of bed or ever worry about what their kids are up to. They have CEO spouses that don't ask them to take out the trash or think about which yogurt to buy.

On the flip side, if they do something bad, that's because they're evil. A deep dark evil totally unlike the banal lameness of the people around us. They don't do stupid shit when someone jerks their chain and they get all worked up. Why would they, they're surrounded by money and other powerful people and have servants feeding them brilliant insights all day long. Everything they do is planned and calculated and they think through the damage they're doing to people in excruciating detail.

There's only one species of humans on Earth, and we're all dumb as shit.

在 HN 中查看
tw04 2026/04/14 14:01
> 当然，我们知道 COMPANY_NAME_HERE 的首席执行官必须一次只穿一条腿，但相似之处仅此而已。

这可能是因为我们有意识或无意识地知道，为了在一家价值数十亿美元的公司中获得并维持权力地位，这个人要么从来没有道德指南针，要么很快就必须找到方法来证明忽视或妥协的合理性。

我们中任何一个曾在这些公司工作过的人都非常有信心，该公司的负责人不会以您描述的方式看待其他人，而是将其视为电子表格中的数字，他们可以通过电子表格中的其他数字来证明自己继续受雇的合理性。

我们大多数人都无法想象以这种方式看待和对待我们的同胞。

> Sure, we know the hotshot CEO of COMPANY_NAME_HERE has to put on his pants one leg at a time, but the similarity ends there.

That’s probably because we know consciously or subconsciously that in order to get and maintain a position of power at a multibillion dollar firm the person either never had a moral compass or quickly had to find ways to justify ignoring or compromising it.

Any one of us who has worked for one of those companies is pretty confident the person running it views other humans not in the way you describe, but as numbers in a spreadsheet who can either justify their continued employment by other numbers in a spreadsheet or not.

Most of us can’t imagine viewing and treating our fellow humans that way.

在 HN 中查看
lmm 2026/04/14 13:54
马斯克现在为 Twitter 支付的价格如何？当然，也许这确实是一个愚蠢的举动，而他只是运气好而已。但他实在是太幸运了。
And how does the price Musk paid for Twitter look now? Sure, maybe it was really a dumb move and he just got lucky. But he's been lucky a hell of a lot.
在 HN 中查看
WatchDog 2026/04/14 14:02
他通过让 xAi 收购 Twitter 来救助他的 Twitter 投资者，然后他通过让 SpaceX 购买 xAi 来救助他的 xAi 投资者，以及他如何试图通过迫使指数基金购买 SpaceX 来救助 SpaceX。
He bailed out his Twitter investors by having xAi purchase Twitter, then he bailed out his xAi investors by having SpaceX purchase xAi, and how he is trying to bail out SpaceX by having the index funds be forced to purchase SpaceX.
在 HN 中查看
qznc 2026/04/14 13:44
对于我的白宫政治运作方式的心智模型，这篇文章非常有影响力： https://www.thewrap.com/obama-aides-say-veep-accurate-west-w...

> “关于《副总统》的有趣之处在于，我们作为在白宫工作的人总是被问到，好吧，什么是最真实的？是《纸牌屋》吗？是《西翼》吗？答案是，这是《副总统》。因为你们抓住了自我的脆弱性，以及日常决策的愚蠢，”维托尔说。

与“阴谋论者是乐观主义者，因为他们相信有一个伟大的计划”的氛围相同。

For my mental model how White House politics work, this article was very influential: https://www.thewrap.com/obama-aides-say-veep-accurate-west-w...

> “The funny thing about ‘Veep’ is, we as people who worked in the White House always get asked, okay, what’s the most real? Is it ‘House of Cards? Is it ‘West Wing’? And the answer is, it’s ‘Veep.’ Because you guys nail the fragility of the egos, and the, like, day-to-day idiocy of the decision-making,” Vietor said.

Same vibe as "conspiracy theorists are optimists because they believe there is a great plan."

Better title: Sometimes people do dumb shit, even powerful people
